source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.4 Organization of a file system (pp. 142-143)

questions.q: What does the FHS standard that Linux follows define?
options: The names and roles of the system's directories
questions.why: The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (see man hier) defines directory names and their roles.

questions.q: Which directory holds files related to system startup (the kernel and boot loader)?
questions.why: /boot contains the files related to system startup.
/etc holds configuration and script files (its name is glossed as 'editable text configuration').

questions.q: Which directory contains files that may change in size, such as spool and log files?
questions.why: /var (variables) holds changing data like spools and logs.

options: A mount point for the proc filesystem, giving information about running processes and the kernel
questions.why: /proc is the proc virtual filesystem exposing process and kernel information; /sys is a similar virtual FS.

questions.q: Which statement about mounting is correct?
options: Mounting onto a non-empty directory hides its contents rather than deleting them, and only the administrator can mount
Mounting onto a non-empty directory deletes its contents
Any user can mount any filesystem
You must be standing under the mount point to unmount it
questions.why: Mounting on a non-empty directory only hides the existing content; only the administrator can mount, and you must not be under a mount point to (un)mount it. Boot-time mounts go in /etc/fstab.
